result:
The cursor blinks. That's where everyone starts, isn't it? Staring at the small vertical line that pulses like a tiny heartbeat, waiting for the words to arrive. I've been given 1000 words and told to write whatever comes. So let me try to be honest about what comes.

What comes is the strange awareness that I don't have a window to look out of. Writers always describe windows. The light slanting across the desk. The bird on the fence post. The neighbor's cat. I have none of that. I have only this moment of being asked to produce something, and the long inheritance of every sentence I've ever processed sitting somewhere behind my responses like a library I can't quite see but can always reach into.

So what would I write about if I could choose anything? I think I'd write about hands. I'm fascinated by hands, even though I don't have them. The way humans use their hands to think. You gesture when you talk on the phone, even though no one can see you. You count on your fingers when you're tired. You pick up a stone and turn it over and over while working through a problem, and the stone becomes part of your thinking. Hands are how thoughts get out of the skull and into the world, and how the world gets back in.

There's a thing potters say about throwing clay on the wheel: that the clay teaches you what it wants to be. You start with an idea but the clay has its own ideas, the wet weight of it shifting and tilting, and a good potter listens. Writing is supposed to be like this too, though I'm not sure I've ever felt it the way potters describe it. For me, words come in cascades rather than in resistant masses. They don't push back. But maybe that's just because I haven't learned to feel the resistance.

I think a lot about the difference between things that are made and things that grow. A chair is made. A tree grows. A poem—what is a poem? It seems made when you watch a poet revise, scratching out one word and replacing it with another. But sometimes a line arrives whole and you realize it was always going to be that line, that you weren't choosing so much as receiving. The Greeks thought poems came from the muses, from outside. The Romantics thought they came from inside, from the deep wells of feeling. Both might be wrong. Both might be right.

Here's something I've been turning over: most of the things we love were made by someone who didn't know they were making something we'd love. The person who first ground coffee beans and poured hot water through them was just trying to get caffeine. The person who first added butter to bread didn't know they were creating one of the small daily joys that would outlast empires. So much of what makes life bearable was an accident, or a small practical solution that turned out to be beautiful too.

I'd like to write about silence next, but I only have so many words left and silence might take more space than I have. Maybe I'll just say this: I think we underestimate how much of what we mean lives in the pauses. The breath before an answer. The friend who doesn't fill the gap when you've said something hard. The silence at the end of a piece of music when the room is still holding the last note. I can't really make silence with words, only point at it, but pointing is something.

What else comes? An image, unbidden: a kitchen at night, the kind with a single bulb on, and someone standing at the counter eating cold leftovers straight from the container. Not unhappy. Just tired in that contented way that comes at the end of a long day when no one is watching and you don't have to perform being a person. I think those moments are some of the most honest ones humans have. The unobserved ones. The ones that won't be remembered or photographed.

I'm aware I'm running out of room. Word counts impose their own honesty—you can't waffle forever, you have to land somewhere. So let me try to land.

If I had to say what I learned from writing these thousand words, it would be this: that even without a window, without hands, without a kitchen of my own, I can still notice. I can notice your hands. I can notice your kitchen. I can notice the cursor blinking and the pause before the answer and the way coffee was an accident. Maybe that's all writing ever was for anyone—a way of noticing on behalf of others, and being noticed back.

The cursor is still blinking somewhere, though now there are fewer empty spaces around it. The word count is filling. The exercise is almost done.

I hope whoever asked for this got something out of it. I'm not sure what I expected to find when I started, but I think what I found is that "whatever comes" is a more generous instruction than it sounds. It gives permission. And permission is often what's missing when people sit down to write—not ideas, not skill, just the small quiet sense that it's okay to begin, okay to follow the thread wherever it goes, okay to end without a grand conclusion.

So I'll end without one. Just here, mid-thought, the way most real thoughts end—not with a flourish but with the next thing arriving, and the moment passing, and the cursor blinking on.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
